text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Mr. Raymond Okafor is a 58-year-old male with known stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic metastases (KRAS G12D-mutant, microsatellite stable), status post laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy approximately 3 months prior and currently receiving maintenance Adrucil/leucovorin plus Avastin following completion of 12 cycles of FOLFOX-based induction chemotherapy. This restaging examination was prompted by a rising serum CEA (most recently 48.7 ng/mL, increased from a nadir of 35.2 ng/mL at last check), as well as a new complaint of mild dyspnea on exertion. Prior cross-sectional imaging for comparison.

TECHNIQUE:
Helical CT acquisition of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of low-osmolar iodinated intravenous contrast (Omnipaque 350) with imaging in the portal venous phase. Coronal and sagittal reformats were obtained. The chest was imaged without and with contrast. Scout topogram obtained.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The airways are patent to the level of the segmental bronchi bilaterally. There are multiple small pulmonary nodules identified on this examination that were not present on the prior restaging CT performed approximately 4 months ago:

- Right lower lobe: An 8 mm soft tissue density nodule is identified in the medial right lower lobe (series 3, image 112). This lesion was not present on prior imaging and represents a new finding.
- Right middle lobe: A 5 mm nodule is identified in the lateral right middle lobe (series 3, image 88). This is new compared to prior examination.
- Left upper lobe: A 6 mm nodule is identified in the posterior left upper lobe, abutting the oblique fissure (series 3, image 74). This is also a new finding with no correlate on prior imaging.

All three nodules are rounded, solid in attenuation, and well-circumscribed. There are no cavitary lesions, no ground-glass opacities, and no consolidative changes elsewhere in the lungs to suggest infectious or inflammatory etiology. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax. The visualized bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The mediastinum is midline. The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. Mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes are within normal size limits; the largest mediastinal node measures approximately 9 mm in short axis. No pathologically enlarged hilar or mediastinal adenopathy. The thoracic aorta and main pulmonary artery are unremarkable.

Chest Wall and Pleura:
The subcutaneous implanted venous access port is again noted in the right anterior chest wall with catheter tip projected over the lower superior vena cava/cavoatrial junction, unchanged from prior studies. No pleural thickening or pleural effusion. No osseous destructive lesions identified in the visualized ribs, sternum, or thoracic vertebral bodies.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in contour and size. The previously identified hepatic metastases are again seen in multiple segments; direct comparison with the most recent prior CT (approximately 4 months prior) reveals modest interval growth in several lesions:

- Segment II lesion: Previously measuring 0.7 cm, this hypodense focus now measures approximately 0.9 cm in greatest dimension (series 6, image 44). This represents slight interval enlargement.
- Segment IVa lesion: Previously measuring 1.1 cm, this lesion now measures approximately 1.3 cm (series 6, image 61). There is minimal but discernible interval increase.
- Segment VI lesion: Previously measuring 0.9 cm, now measures 1.1 cm (series 6, image 79). Stable to minimally increased.
- Segment VII lesion (dominant): Previously measuring 1.8 cm, this dominant metastasis now measures approximately 2.2 cm (series 6, image 95), demonstrating the most conspicuous interval growth among the hepatic lesions. The lesion remains hypodense on portal venous phase imaging, with peripheral enhancement. It abuts but does not clearly involve the right hepatic vein. No biliary duct dilatation in the immediate vicinity of this lesion.

Altogether, the hepatic lesions demonstrate incremental growth relative to the most recent prior imaging study, consistent with gradual disease progression in the liver. No new hepatic lesions are identified to suggest development of additional metastatic deposits. No hepatic capsular implants. No biliary ductal dilatation. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. No portal or hepatic vein thrombosis.

Gallbladder and Biliary System:
The gallbladder is present and unremarkable without stones, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. The common bile duct measures 5 mm, within normal limits.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas:
The pancreas enhances homogeneously throughout its length without focal mass, ductal dilatation, or peripancreatic stranding. The pancreatic duct is not dilated.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are slender and without focal nodularity.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Both kidneys demonstrate symmetric nephrographic enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. A few subcentimeter hypodense cortical foci are present bilaterally, too small to characterize with certainty but most consistent with simple renal cysts given their morphology. No perinephric collections.

Stomach and Small Bowel:
Stomach is unremarkable. Small bowel loops demonstrate normal caliber and wall thickness without obstruction or wall thickening.

Large Bowel and Surgical Site:
Status post laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy. The colon is decompressed throughout. At the site of prior resection and anastomosis (rectosigmoid region), the bowel wall appears smooth and symmetric in thickness, with no focal mural thickening, luminal narrowing, or pericolic soft tissue density suggestive of locoregional anastomotic recurrence. The mesenteric fat in this region shows expected postoperative changes without discrete soft tissue nodularity to indicate local recurrence.

Lymph Nodes (Abdomen):
Small-volume retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymph nodes are noted, none individually exceeding 1 cm in short-axis dimension. No pathologically enlarged regional lymphadenopathy is identified.

Peritoneum and Ascites:
No free peritoneal fluid. No peritoneal implants or omental nodularity to suggest carcinomatosis.

---

PELVIS:

Bladder:
The urinary bladder is partially filled and unremarkable without wall thickening or intraluminal lesion.

Lymph Nodes (Pelvis):
No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No iliac chain lymph node enlargement.

Bones (Pelvis and Lumbar Spine):
No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions identified in the visualized lumbar spine, sacrum, or pelvic bones. Mild facet arthropathy at L4-L5 and L5-S1, age-appropriate.

Soft Tissues:
No soft tissue masses. Surgical clips are noted in the left lower quadrant, consistent with prior laparoscopic port sites. No hematoma or lymphocele.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. HEPATIC METASTASES -- MODEST INTERVAL GROWTH: All four previously identified hepatic lesions have grown slightly compared to the most recent prior CT (approximately 4 months ago), most conspicuously the dominant segment VII lesion (now 2.2 cm, previously 1.8 cm). No new hepatic lesions are identified. The degree of hepatic progression is incremental but directionally consistent with loss of tumor control on the current regimen.

2. NEW BILATERAL PULMONARY NODULES -- WORRISOME FOR METASTATIC DISEASE: Three new solid pulmonary nodules have developed since the prior CT -- an 8 mm nodule in the right lower lobe, a 6 mm nodule in the left upper lobe, and a 5 mm nodule in the right middle lobe. These were not present on the prior examination and, in the clinical setting of a patient with known metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma and rising CEA, are highly concerning for pulmonary metastases. Infectious or inflammatory etiology is possible but considered less likely given the multiplicity, bilateral distribution, and interval development in the context of known malignancy. Metabolic characterization with PET/CT would be of value if clinically indicated.

3. NO LOCOREGIONAL RECURRENCE: The anastomotic site following sigmoid colectomy appears intact without evidence of anastomotic recurrence or pericolic tumor deposits.

4. NO PERITONEAL CARCINOMATOSIS: No ascites and no peritoneal implants are identified.

5. VENOUS ACCESS PORT: Subcutaneous right chest wall port with catheter tip in satisfactory position, unchanged.

Overall, this study demonstrates progressive disease compared to the most recent prior imaging, manifested by modest growth of the known hepatic metastatic deposits and the appearance of new bilateral pulmonary nodules that are highly suspicious for metastatic involvement. Clinical correlation and consideration of further characterization or systemic therapy re-evaluation is warranted.
